characters.relation: Gallic chief who took Rome
characters.blurb: Led the Senones that crushed Rome at the Allia and held the city for ransom until Camillus returned as dictator.

characters.relation: Etruscan city he finally captured
characters.blurb: Rome’s rich rival across the Tiber. Camillus took it after a long siege—by tunnel into Juno’s temple, in the famous tale.

characters.relation: Goddess brought from Veii to Rome
characters.blurb: Juno Regina nodded (they say) when asked to go to Rome; Camillus’s evocatio made her a Roman goddess.

characters.relation: Saved the Capitol; later rival
characters.blurb: Heard the sacred geese and held the Tarpeian rock against the Gauls; later courted the debtors and fell as a demagogue.

characters.relation: Manlius’s surname from the rock
characters.blurb: Title of Marcus Manlius after defending the Capitol—glory that later curdled into sedition against Camillus’s order.

characters.relation: People of Falerii; schoolmaster tale
characters.blurb: When their schoolmaster betrayed the boys to Camillus, he returned the traitor unbound and won the city by justice.

characters.relation: Camillus’s gentile name
characters.blurb: Marcus Furius Camillus—five times dictator—the man Plutarch paints as a second Romulus after the Gallic sack.

characters.relation: Founder Camillus is likened to
characters.blurb: After the sack some urged abandoning Rome; Camillus argued like a re-founder that the site itself was sacred.

characters.relation: River of the Gallic disaster
characters.blurb: Stream where the Gauls destroyed the Roman army on a dies ater still marked in the calendar.

characters.relation: Ally Camillus rescued
characters.blurb: Etruscan-facing town taken and retaken in the wars; Camillus restored it in a single day’s hard fighting.

characters.relation: City of his exile; base of return
characters.blurb: Camillus was living in exile there when the Gauls came; from Ardea he gathered strength to relieve Rome.

characters.relation: Messenger who swam the Tiber
characters.blurb: Carried word through the enemy to the Capitol—and brought the Senate’s recall of Camillus as dictator.

characters.relation: Heard the night warning
characters.blurb: Plebeian who reported a divine voice in the Via Nova foretelling the Gallic onset—ignored until too late.

characters.relation: Clan prominent in the Veientine war
characters.blurb: Their private war at the Cremera and later commands frame the generation Camillus dominated.

characters.relation: Negotiated the Gallic ransom
characters.blurb: Tribune or commander who weighed the thousand pounds of gold before Camillus forbade the shameful bargain.

characters.relation: Italian enemies after the sack
characters.blurb: Pressed Rome while she was weak; Camillus’s dictatorships repeatedly broke their invasions.

characters.relation: Hill foes allied against Rome
characters.blurb: Fought beside Volscians in the post-Gallic wars Camillus was called to master.

characters.relation: Etruscan enemies beyond Veii
characters.blurb: Broader Etruscan front—Capena, Falerii, and others—against which Camillus earned his early fame.

characters.relation: Colleagues and kinsmen in command
characters.blurb: Several Lucii share Camillus’s Fasti as consular tribunes and fellow generals in the long Veientine struggle.

characters.relation: Dictator under whom Camillus first served; rival magistrate
characters.blurb: Postumius Tubertus was dictator in the great battle against the Aequians and Volscians where the young Camillus first distinguished himself; a later Postumius appears among Camillus's awkward colleagues when politics cut short a command or triumph.

characters.relation: Peaceful king invoked after the sack
characters.blurb: When Romans debated moving to Veii, Camillus’s speech leaned on Numa’s religious founding of the city’s sites.

characters.relation: Town whose quarrel brought the Gauls
characters.blurb: Etruscan city that invited Roman envoys against the Gauls—and so drew Brennus south on Rome.

characters.relation: Clusian who fetched the Gauls
characters.blurb: Lucumo wronged at Clusium who guided the Gallic mercenaries into Etruria—and toward Rome.

characters.relation: Tribune of the people; reforming land-law agitator
characters.blurb: Raised a great sedition demanding that no one hold more than five hundred acres; the senate needed a dictator to pass the law, then caught Stolo himself violating it.

characters.relation: Envoy and soldier in the Gallic crisis
characters.blurb: Fabius Ambustus was one of the envoys who visited Delphi before the Veii siege; Quintus Ambustus, well-mounted, charged a Gaul in the hill skirmish that drew the Senones south.

characters.relation: Devout citizen who rescued the Vestal Virgins
characters.blurb: Traveling by cart with his family during the Gallic rout, he made his own family walk so the Vestals and their sacred objects could ride to safety at Caere.

characters.relation: Senator who struck a Gaul with his staff
characters.blurb: Old senator sitting still in his chair in the Forum when the Gauls entered; struck a Gaul who stroked his beard, and was the first to die in the sack of the city.

characters.relation: Handmaid who saved Rome from the Latins by deception
characters.blurb: Called Philotis or Tutula, this slave-woman persuaded the magistrates to send her and other handmaids disguised as free women into the Latin camp; she lit the signal fire.

characters.relation: Magistrate who voted first on moving to Veii
characters.blurb: Senior officer whose turn it was to speak first when Rome debated abandoning the burned city for Veii; a centurion’s chance command interrupted him as a divine sign.

characters.relation: Philosopher-historian who recorded the Gallic sack in Greece
characters.blurb: Wrote of a great army from the far west destroying a Greek city called Rome—Plutarch’s earliest Greek evidence for the sack, though he calls Heraclides high-flown.

characters.relation: Trojan ancestor invoked in the debate on Rome’s site
characters.blurb: Plutarch notes that the Palladium and Samothracian images were said to have come with Aeneas to Italy—relics stored in the inmost shrine whose rites Numa founded.

characters.relation: Macedonian king compared to Camillus in hypothetical match
characters.blurb: Plutarch entertains the conjecture of what would have happened had Alexander marched west and met Rome—Camillus’s Rome is the implied Roman champion.

characters.relation: Camillus's master of horse for the Veii campaign
characters.blurb: Chosen by Camillus as his cavalry commander when appointed dictator to end the long siege of Veii; the appointment marks the campaign that launched Camillus's reputation as Rome's greatest general.

characters.relation: Ambassador to Delphi before the fall of Veii
characters.blurb: One of three distinguished envoys sent to consult Apollo at Delphi about the portent of the Alban lake; they returned with the oracle's instructions that allowed the siege of Veii to continue.

characters.relation: Ambassador to Delphi before the fall of Veii
characters.blurb: Sent with Licinius Cossus and Fabius Ambustus to consult Apollo at Delphi; the mission resolved the mystery of the Alban lake and cleared the way for Camillus's final assault on Veii.

characters.relation: Liparean general who freed the Roman envoys
characters.blurb: Commander at the Liparean islands who intervened when his men seized the Roman delegation as suspected pirates; by his authority and persuasion the embassy was dismissed unharmed and accompanied toward Delphi.

characters.relation: Accuser who drove Camillus into exile
characters.blurb: Prosecuted Camillus for allegedly retaining Tuscan spoils—including brass gates—from the sack of Veii; his charge, backed by popular anger over the tenths controversy, forced Camillus to depart into exile at Ardea.

characters.relation: Censor whose death foretold the Gallic disaster
characters.blurb: The Roman censor Julius died just before Camillus's exile; Plutarch marks this as the first omen of the coming catastrophe, since the censorship was held sacred and its holder's death an evil portent.

characters.relation: Ward of Aruns whose adultery brought the Gauls
characters.blurb: A wealthy and handsome orphan raised in Aruns's household at Clusium; his affair with Aruns's wife and the lawsuit that humiliated Aruns drove Aruns to invite the Gallic mercenaries into Italy, setting in motion the sack of Rome.

characters.relation: Dictator who imprisoned Manlius
characters.blurb: Made dictator to suppress the popular disorders fomented by Marcus Manlius; had Manlius imprisoned, though the people immediately put on mourning and the senate ordered his release.

characters.relation: Rash colleague who nearly lost a battle
characters.blurb: Camillus's fellow military tribune who, impatient for glory and against Camillus's counsel, rashly engaged the Volscians and was routed; Camillus leaped from his sickbed to rally the fugitives and win the battle himself.

characters.relation: Patrician consul in the first election under the Licinian law
characters.blurb: Chosen by Camillus in the consular election that marked the end of his last dictatorship; the patrician of the new pair, standing alongside Lucius Sextius as the first plebeian consul.

characters.relation: First plebeian consul
characters.blurb: Elected in the consular election that Camillus conducted as the final act of his dictatorship; the first man of plebeian birth to hold the consulship, a constitutional landmark the Licinian law made possible.

characters.relation: Roman historian cited on the evocatio of Juno
characters.blurb: Plutarch notes that Livy gives a somewhat different account of the moment Juno's statue was invited to Rome—where Camillus touched the goddess and bystanders cried out that she was willing—while Plutarch follows the version that the statue answered in a low voice.

characters.relation: Philosopher who recorded the Gallic sack but named the wrong deliverer
characters.blurb: Aristotle apparently heard a correct account of the sack of Rome by the Gauls, but Plutarch notes that Aristotle calls Rome's deliverer 'Lucius' — whereas Camillus's forename was Marcus, not Lucius.
